# CPE Accuracy Test Data

This directory holds golden-file inputs for the `cpe-accuracy` CLI tool.

## Running the tool

First, populate a vuln data directory (reuses Fleet's canonical downloader):

```
fleetctl vulnerability-data-stream --dir /tmp/vulnsdb
```

Then run the tool:

```
go run ./tools/nvd/accuracy/cpe-accuracy --vuln-path /tmp/vulnsdb
```

The tool also works without `--vuln-path` -- it falls back to `$NVD_TEST_VULNDB_DIR`,
and if that is unset it downloads into a temp directory (slower first run).

Each `cves_*.json` file is an "accuracy suite" that binds real macOS software
inputs (as reported by osquery) to the CPE string Fleet's fuzzy matcher should
produce. The tool feeds each software input through `nvd.TranslateSoftwareToCPE`
against the real `cpe.sqlite` + `cpe_translations.json`, then classifies each case:

| Result                | Meaning                                             |
|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| `PASS`                | expected non-empty CPE and got an exact match       |
| `PASS (true negative)`| expected empty CPE and got empty                    |
| `FAIL (mismatch)`     | expected non-empty CPE but got a different CPE      |
| `FAIL (missing)`      | expected non-empty CPE but got empty                |
| `FAIL (false positive)`| expected empty CPE but got a non-empty CPE         |

## File format

```json
{
  "metadata": {
    "generated_at": "2026-04-15T00:00:00Z",
    "platform": "macos",
    "year": 2026,
    "severity": "CRITICAL",
    "description": "..."
  },
  "test_cases": [
    {
      "id": "unique-case-id",
      "software": {
        "name": "Firefox.app",
        "version": "125.0",
        "source": "apps",
        "bundle_identifier": "org.mozilla.firefox"
      },
      "expected_cpe": "cpe:2.3:a:mozilla:firefox:125.0:*:*:*:*:macos:*:*",
      "source_cve": "CVE-2026-12345",
      "cvss_score": 9.8,
      "tags": ["browser"],
      "notes": "Optional commentary for reviewers"
    }
  ]
}
```

`expected_cpe` set to the empty string encodes "this input should NOT produce a
CPE" -- use it to pin false-positive guards (helper apps, uninstaller bundles,
known-bad matches).

## File naming

- `cves_2026_critical_apps.json` — macOS `.app` test cases
- `cves_2026_critical_homebrew.json` — Homebrew CLI test cases
- `cves_2026_critical_extensions.json` — Browser and IDE extensions
- `cves_2026_critical_language_pkgs.json` — `python_packages`, `npm_packages`

Any file matching `cves_*.json` is picked up automatically.

## Refresh

The golden files are generated by three tools under `tools/nvd/accuracy/`
composed into a small loop:

```
fleetctl vulnerability-data-stream --dir /tmp/vulnsdb      # refresh NVD feeds
            │
            ▼
./tools/nvd/accuracy/cpe-candidates                        # candidates JSON
            │
            ├──► ./tools/nvd/accuracy/recipe-generator     # install on VM
            │         (Claude + SSH, idempotent)
            │
            └──► ./tools/nvd/accuracy/testdata-reconcile   # query VM + write
                      (SSH osquery + matcher)                goldens + missing
                                                             products list
```

1. **`cpe-candidates`** reads 2026 CRITICAL CVEs from local NVD feeds in
   `/tmp/vulnsdb` and emits `cpe_candidates_<year>_<severity>.json` — a list
   of macOS-relevant vendor/product pairs.

2. **`recipe-generator`** consumes that candidates file (or
   `missing_products.json` from reconcile). For each product without a
   `verified` recipe it either uses a Homebrew formula shortcut or shells
   out to the Claude Code CLI to research the install method, then SSHes
   to the macOS VM and actually installs the software. Records the recipe
   + validation status in `install_recipes.json`. Idempotent across runs.

3. **`testdata-reconcile`** reads the candidates, SSHes to the VM, runs one
   `osqueryd -S --json '<query>'` per source table, and writes the final
   golden files:
   - `cves_<year>_<severity>_apps.json` — macOS `.app` matches
   - `cves_<year>_<severity>_homebrew.json` — Homebrew CLI / cask matches
   - `cves_<year>_<severity>_extensions.json` — browser/IDE extensions
   - `cves_<year>_<severity>_language_pkgs.json` — python / npm packages
   - `missing_products.json` — candidates the VM doesn't have yet (feeds
     directly into `recipe-generator --input` for the next loop iteration).

Reconcile queries the VM every run (~15s). There is no persisted osquery
snapshot — the golden files themselves are the durable artifact.

Typical refresh loop:

```sh
fleetctl vulnerability-data-stream --dir /tmp/vulnsdb
go run ./tools/nvd/accuracy/cpe-candidates
go run ./tools/nvd/accuracy/recipe-generator --max-budget-usd 20
go run ./tools/nvd/accuracy/testdata-reconcile
# If missing_products.json is non-empty, loop:
go run ./tools/nvd/accuracy/recipe-generator \
    --input server/vulnerabilities/nvd/testdata/accuracy/missing_products.json
go run ./tools/nvd/accuracy/testdata-reconcile
```

The generated `cves_*.json` files are checked into git and treated as the
stable baseline; they evolve as new CRITICAL CVEs land and as more products
get installed on the VM.
